description: The real thing. Smoky, deeply seasoned, party-style jollof rice cooked the traditional way — long-fried tomato base, layered spices, and that iconic bottom-pot char.

ingredients.name: parboiled long grain rice
ingredients.note: Parboiled only. Regular rice will go mushy. Wash until water runs completely clear before cooking.

ingredients.name: plum tomatoes
ingredients.note: Fresh is better for party jollof. Canned works but fry longer to cook out the tinny taste.

ingredients.name: red bell pepper (tatashe)
ingredients.note: Gives the deep red colour and mild sweetness. Do not skip.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper (ata rodo)
ingredients.note: 1 for mild, 2 for medium, 3 for real party heat. Adjust to your tolerance.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 2 onions go into the blended base. 1 onion is sliced into rings for frying.

ingredients.name: vegetable oil
ingredients.note: Do not reduce this. The oil carries the flavour through the entire dish.

ingredients.name: chicken stock
ingredients.note: Homemade is best. A cube dissolved in hot water is acceptable. Beef stock also works.

ingredients.name: tomato paste
ingredients.note: Deepens the colour and concentrates the tomato flavour. Do not skip.

ingredients.name: bay leaves
ingredients.note: Adds background herbal depth. Remove before serving — do not eat.

ingredients.name: curry powder
ingredients.note: Nigerian curry powder — the yellow tin. This is not optional for party jollof flavour.

ingredients.name: thyme
ingredients.note: Dried thyme is fine. Fresh thyme (2 sprigs) also works.

ingredients.name: white pepper
ingredients.note: Adds warmth without visible specks. Black pepper substitutes fine.

ingredients.name: ground crayfish
ingredients.note: Gives authentic umami depth. Skip if unavailable — the dish still works without it.

ingredients.name: seasoning cubes
ingredients.note: Crush both cubes into powder before adding so they dissolve evenly.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Season in two stages — once when making the base, once before adding the rice.

ingredients.name: chicken pieces
ingredients.note: For full party jollof — parboil or grill the chicken and use the stock. Skip for vegetarian.

ingredients.name: foil or heavy lid
ingredients.quantity: enough to seal the pot tightly
ingredients.note: Critical. Foil pressed directly onto the rice surface under the lid traps steam. This is the secret to the smoky party bottom.

steps.title: Prepare the Tomato Base
steps.instruction: Blend your tomatoes, red bell peppers, scotch bonnet peppers, and 2 of the onions until completely smooth. You want a thick, vibrant red liquid with zero chunks. Pour it into a bowl and set aside. Take your third onion and slice it into thin rings.
micro_steps: Wash and roughly chop 6 medium plum tomatoes — no need to remove seeds
Wash, deseed and roughly chop 2 large red bell peppers (tatashe)
Wash 2–3 scotch bonnet peppers — leave whole, seeds and all, for heat
Peel and roughly chop 2 of the 3 large onions
Add all of the above into a blender — do not add water
Blend on high until completely smooth — about 60 seconds
Pour the blend into a bowl and set aside
Peel the third onion and slice it into thin rings — set aside separately
steps.sensory_cue: Deep red, completely smooth, thick liquid. Should smell sharp and fresh. If you see chunks, blend again.
steps.failure_risk: If the blend is very watery, briefly strain it or you will spend much longer frying it down. Too much water means weak, sour base.
prep_notes: Have a large bowl ready to receive the blended mix
Measure out all dry spices before you start cooking — things move fast once the heat is on
If using chicken, it should already be parboiled and the stock reserved

steps.title: Fry the Sliced Onions
steps.instruction: Pour the vegetable oil into a large, heavy-bottomed pot — a cast iron or thick aluminium pot is ideal. Heat over medium heat until the oil shimmers. Add your sliced onion rings and fry, stirring occasionally, until they are completely soft, translucent, and beginning to turn golden at the edges. This is your first flavour layer.
micro_steps: Pour ½ cup (120ml) vegetable oil into a large, heavy-bottomed pot
Heat the oil over medium heat for about 90 seconds — it should shimmer when you tilt the pot
Add all the sliced onion rings into the hot oil
Stir once to coat the onions in the oil
Fry, stirring every 30 seconds, for 3–4 minutes
Stop when the onions look soft, slightly translucent, and the edges are just beginning to turn golden
steps.sensory_cue: Onions should look soft and smell sweet — not raw and sharp. Oil should shimmer around them. The edges should be just beginning to colour.
steps.failure_risk: Do not burn the onions. Burnt onion at this stage makes the entire dish taste bitter no matter what you do after. If they start browning too fast, reduce the heat immediately.

steps.title: Add Tomato Paste and Fry
steps.instruction: Add the tomato paste directly into the fried onions in the pot. Stir it in immediately and fry, stirring constantly, for 3–4 minutes until it darkens from bright red to a deeper brick-red and smells cooked rather than raw or tinny. This step removes the tinny, raw paste taste.
micro_steps: Spoon 3 level tablespoons of tomato paste into the pot with the fried onions
Stir immediately and vigorously — the paste will try to stick
Keep stirring continuously over medium heat
Watch the colour — it should shift from bright orange-red to a deeper, darker brick-red
After 3–4 minutes of stirring, smell it — the sharp, tinny smell should be gone, replaced by something richer
The paste is ready when it looks slightly caramelised at the edges and smells cooked
steps.sensory_cue: Paste darkens from bright red to deeper brick-red. Smell shifts from sharp and tinny to rich and slightly caramelised.
steps.failure_risk: Under-frying the paste leaves a tinny, industrial taste in the finished dish. You must cook this out completely. If it sticks, add a tiny splash of oil.

steps.title: Pour In Blended Mix and Fry the Base
steps.instruction: Pour your blended tomato and pepper mix into the pot with the fried paste. Stir everything together to combine. Increase the heat to high. Now fry this mix — stirring every 90 seconds — until the liquid is dramatically reduced, the colour has deepened to a dark orange-red, and the oil has fully risen and floated visibly to the surface. This step is the entire heart of party jollof. Do not rush it.
micro_steps: Pour the entire blended tomato-pepper mix into the pot — stand back, it may splatter
Stir thoroughly to combine the blend with the fried paste and onions
Increase heat to high
Set a timer for 20 minutes — this is your minimum frying time
Every 90 seconds, stir from the bottom of the pot to prevent sticking
After 10 minutes, the mix should look noticeably thicker and darker
After 20 minutes, check: the oil should be floating clearly on the surface — visible orange pools of oil
The mix should look paste-like, not liquid — almost like a thick sauce
The raw tomato smell should be 100% gone — it should smell rich, fried, and deeply savoury
If the oil has not risen yet, continue frying for another 5–10 minutes
steps.sensory_cue: Base should look thick and paste-like, not liquid. Oil visibly floating on top. Colour: deep brick-red to dark orange. The raw tomato smell must be completely gone — replaced by a rich, fried aroma.
steps.failure_risk: This is the most common party jollof failure point. Insufficient frying leaves a watery, sour, raw-tasting base. The rice will cook unevenly and the dish will taste wrong. The oil rising is your only reliable signal that the base is done. Do not move past this step until you see the oil on top.

steps.title: Season the Base
steps.instruction: Reduce the heat to medium. Now add all your dry spices and seasoning directly into the fried base and stir everything in thoroughly. Taste the base — it should taste well-seasoned, almost slightly over-salted (the rice will absorb and dilute the salt). Add the bay leaves and stir in. This is your last real seasoning window.
micro_steps: Reduce heat from high to medium
Add 2 tsp curry powder — stir in
Add 1½ tsp dried thyme — stir in
Add 1 tsp white pepper — stir in
Add 1 tbsp ground crayfish if using — stir in
Crush 2 Maggi or Knorr cubes into a powder between your fingers and add — stir in
Add salt — start with ½ tsp, stir, then taste
The base should taste bold, full, and slightly over-seasoned — this is correct, the rice will mellow it
Add 3 bay leaves — press them down into the base
Stir everything one final time to make sure all spices are evenly distributed
steps.sensory_cue: Base should smell complex, layered — curry and thyme clearly distinct. Colour shifts to warm, deep orange-red. Taste it — it should be bold and full. If it tastes flat, add more seasoning now.
steps.failure_risk: Under-seasoning at this stage cannot be meaningfully fixed after the rice goes in. Be bold with the seasoning here.

steps.title: Add Chicken Stock
steps.instruction: Pour the chicken stock into the seasoned base. Stir to fully combine. Increase the heat to medium-high and bring everything to a rolling boil. Taste the liquid one more time — it should be deeply flavoured, almost like a very good soup. Adjust salt if needed right now, before the rice goes in.
micro_steps: Pour 2 cups (500ml) of chicken stock into the pot
Stir vigorously to combine the stock with the base
Increase heat to medium-high
Wait for the liquid to reach a proper rolling boil — you should see active bubbling across the surface
Taste the liquid now — dip a spoon and taste carefully
If it tastes flat or weak, add another half a seasoning cube crumbled in and stir
If it needs more salt, add a pinch and stir
The liquid should taste deeply savoury and full — like the finished dish already
steps.sensory_cue: The pot should be bubbling actively. Colour: rich orange-red. Aroma: full, savoury, complex — like the dish is almost already done.
steps.failure_risk: Weak or cold stock dilutes all the work you did on the base. If your stock tastes bland, add another seasoning cube now. Once the rice goes in, this cannot be corrected.

steps.title: Add the Rice
steps.instruction: Add your washed parboiled rice to the boiling base. Stir well so every single grain gets coated in the orange sauce. Check the liquid level — it should just about reach the surface of the rice. If it doesn't, add a small splash of hot water or stock. Do not add too much. Stir one more time, then reduce the heat to low.
micro_steps: Measure 4 cups of parboiled rice — make sure it has been washed until the water runs clear
Pour all the rice into the boiling base
Stir immediately and thoroughly — every grain should be coated in the orange-red base
Look at the liquid level — it should be just at or barely below the surface of the rice
If the liquid is more than 2cm below the rice surface, add ¼ cup of hot water and stir
If the liquid is sitting above the rice, you have too much — let it boil for 2 more minutes before adding the rice
Stir one final time from the bottom up
Reduce heat to low
steps.sensory_cue: Rice should look evenly coated — every grain stained orange-red. Liquid should be at or just below the surface of the rice, not drowning it.
steps.failure_risk: Too much liquid produces soggy, wet jollof. Too little and the rice won't cook through before the liquid evaporates and the bottom burns. The liquid level at the rice surface is the target.

steps.title: Seal and Steam — First Cook
steps.instruction: Take your foil and press it directly onto the surface of the rice — it should be in contact with the rice itself, not just covering the pot. Then put the lid on top of the foil to create a tight seal. Cook on the lowest heat setting your stove has. Do NOT open the lid for the first 20 minutes. Let the steam do the work.
micro_steps: Tear a piece of foil large enough to cover the entire pot opening
Press the foil directly onto the surface of the rice — it should touch the rice, not float above it
Fold the foil over the edges of the pot to seal the sides
Place the heavy lid on top of the foil
Reduce heat to the lowest setting on your stove
Set a timer for 20 minutes
Do not touch the pot, do not open it, do not peek
After about 12–15 minutes you may smell a faint smoky aroma — this is the party bottom forming, it is correct
steps.sensory_cue: You should see steam escaping from the edges of the lid. After 15 minutes, a slightly smoky smell will develop from the bottom — this is the party jollof bottom. This is exactly right.
steps.failure_risk: Lifting the lid releases steam and causes uneven cooking — some rice will be hard, some mushy. The only exception: if you smell actual burning (bitter, acrid) rather than the pleasant smoky char smell, reduce heat immediately.

steps.title: Check, Fold and Finish
steps.instruction: After 20 minutes, carefully open the lid — steam will rush out, so tilt the lid away from your face. Check the rice: it should be almost fully done with just a slight firmness in the centre of each grain. Using a wooden spoon, gently fold the rice once — scoop from the bottom and bring it to the top. This distributes the smoky bottom layer through the whole pot. Re-seal with foil and lid. Cook for a final 10–15 minutes on the lowest heat.
micro_steps: After exactly 20 minutes, carefully lift the lid away from your face — steam burns
Remove the foil carefully — again, away from your face
Take a spoon and press a few grains of rice — they should feel almost soft with just the tiniest firmness left
Using a wooden spoon, go to the very bottom of the pot and gently scoop upward
Fold the bottom rice to the top in one slow motion — do this once only
Check the bottom — there should be a thin, slightly darkened crust, not black char
If the bottom looks very dark, reduce your heat even further before re-sealing
Place fresh foil back on the surface of the rice
Put the lid back on
Cook for a final 10 minutes on the lowest possible heat
steps.sensory_cue: The bottom layer should have a thin, slightly darkened crust — the smoky party bottom. It should smell deeply savoury and slightly charred. The folded rice should look fluffy and fully coated. After the final 10 minutes, grains should be completely soft with no hard centre.
steps.failure_risk: Over-folding breaks the rice grains and creates mush. One slow, gentle fold from bottom to top is all you do. If the bottom is already black when you check, your heat was too high — reduce it before the final seal.

steps.title: Rest and Serve
steps.instruction: Turn off the heat completely. Keep the pot sealed with foil and lid. Let the jollof rest for 5–10 minutes before opening. This allows the steam to redistribute evenly and the rice to settle. When you open it, remove all the bay leaves — find every single one. Fluff gently with a fork from the edges inward before plating.
micro_steps: Turn the heat off completely
Keep the foil and lid on — do not open yet
Set a timer for 5 minutes minimum, 10 minutes is better
After resting, open carefully — face away from the steam
Scan the surface for bay leaves and remove every one — check the sides and bottom too
Use a fork to gently fluff from the edges of the pot inward — do not stir aggressively
Look at the rice — each grain should be separate, fluffy, and stained a deep orange-red
Smell it — smoky, deeply savoury, and unmistakably Nigerian party jollof
Plate and serve immediately
steps.sensory_cue: Rice should look fluffy with each grain separate and stained a rich orange-red. The aroma rising from the pot should be smoky, deeply savoury, and complex. This is the moment — it should smell exactly like every party you have ever been to.
steps.failure_risk: Serving immediately without resting causes the rice to clump and the steam to escape unevenly — some grains will still be slightly firm. Five minutes of resting makes a real, visible difference to the texture.
